description: Bloodborne Pathogens (BBP) training covering OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1030, Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, HIV, universal precautions, engineering controls, PPE requirements, exposure incident procedures, and post-exposure evaluation.

questions.q: What are bloodborne pathogens?
questions.a: Pathogenic microorganisms (viruses, bacteria, parasites) present in human blood and other potentially infectious materials (OPIM) that can cause disease in humans.

questions.q: What are the three bloodborne pathogens of greatest concern?
questions.a: Hepatitis B virus (HBV), Hepatitis C virus (HCV), and Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV).

questions.q: What OSHA standard covers bloodborne pathogens?
questions.a: 29 CFR 1910.1030 — the OSHA Bloodborne Pathogens Standard.

questions.q: Is blood the only bodily fluid that can contain bloodborne pathogens?
questions.a: No. Pathogens can also be transmitted through semen, vaginal secretions, cerebrospinal fluid, synovial fluid, pleural fluid, amniotic fluid, saliva in dental procedures, and breast milk.

questions.q: Which bloodborne pathogen is NOT transmitted through blood: Hepatitis B, HIV, or Influenza?
questions.a: Influenza — it is transmitted via respiratory droplets, not through blood or bodily fluids.

questions.q: What are universal precautions?
questions.a: An approach to infection control where all human blood and certain body fluids are treated as if known to be infectious for HIV, HBV, and other bloodborne pathogens.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of PPE in the context of bloodborne pathogens?
questions.a: To create a barrier between the worker and potentially infectious materials, protecting the worker from infection.

questions.q: What types of PPE are used for bloodborne pathogen protection?
questions.a: Gloves, gowns, face shields, masks, eye protection (goggles or safety glasses with side shields), and mouthpieces/resuscitation bags.

questions.q: Is it safe to recap needles after use if done carefully?
questions.a: No. Recapping needles is one of the leading causes of accidental needlestick injuries and is prohibited unless using a mechanical device or one-handed technique.

questions.q: How often must bloodborne pathogens training be provided to employees?
questions.a: At the time of initial assignment and at least annually thereafter.

questions.q: Is a vaccination available for Hepatitis B?
questions.a: Yes. The Hepatitis B vaccine is highly effective and must be offered free of charge to employees with occupational exposure within 10 working days of initial assignment.

questions.q: Is there a vaccine for Hepatitis C or HIV?
questions.a: No. There is currently no vaccine available for Hepatitis C or HIV.

questions.q: What is a common symptom of Hepatitis B infection?
questions.a: Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), along with fatigue, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, nausea, and dark urine.

questions.q: Do protective gloves eliminate the need for handwashing after blood exposure?
questions.a: No. Hands must be washed with soap and water immediately after removing gloves, regardless of whether glove integrity was maintained.

questions.q: What is NOT recommended after a needlestick injury?
questions.a: Squeezing the wound — this can increase the risk of infection by forcing pathogens deeper into tissue.

questions.q: What is the correct procedure for cleaning up a blood spill?
questions.a: Contain the spill, apply an EPA-registered disinfectant (such as a 10% bleach solution), clean with absorbent material, dispose of waste in a biohazard bag, and wash hands.

questions.q: Can bloodborne pathogens only be transmitted through visible blood?
questions.a: No. Pathogens can be present in body fluids even without visible blood contamination.

questions.q: How often must an Exposure Control Plan be updated?
questions.a: At least annually, to reflect changes in tasks, procedures, and technology that eliminate or reduce occupational exposure.

questions.q: Do only direct-contact healthcare workers need bloodborne pathogen training?
questions.a: No. All employees who have reasonably anticipated occupational exposure to blood or OPIM require training, regardless of their specific role.

questions.q: Approximately how many known bloodborne pathogens exist?
questions.a: At least 20 known bloodborne pathogens, though HBV, HCV, and HIV are the three of greatest concern.

questions.q: What is NOT a transmission route for bloodborne pathogens in the workplace?
questions.a: Sneezing — bloodborne pathogens are transmitted through contact with infected blood or body fluids, not through respiratory droplets.

questions.q: What is secondary transmission?
questions.a: When a healthcare provider or worker infected with a bloodborne pathogen subsequently passes the infection to another person.

questions.q: What type of exposure incident is most likely to result in transmission of a bloodborne pathogen?
questions.a: A needlestick (percutaneous) injury.

questions.q: How long can Hepatitis B virus survive in dried blood on surfaces?
questions.a: Up to one week (7 days) at room temperature.

questions.q: What percentage of needlestick injuries can result in Hepatitis B transmission if the person is not vaccinated?
questions.a: 6-30%, depending on the source patient's HBeAg status.

questions.q: What is the risk of HCV infection following percutaneous exposure?
questions.a: Approximately 1.8% (less than 2%).

questions.q: What is an Exposure Control Plan (ECP)?
questions.a: A written plan required by OSHA that describes the employer's strategy for eliminating or minimizing employee exposure to bloodborne pathogens, including exposure determination, methods of compliance, and post-exposure procedures.

questions.q: What are engineering controls in the context of bloodborne pathogens?
questions.a: Devices that isolate or remove the bloodborne pathogen hazard from the workplace, such as self-sheathing needles, sharps disposal containers, needleless IV systems, and splash guards.

questions.q: Are disposable gloves considered an engineering control?
questions.a: No. Disposable gloves are personal protective equipment (PPE), not an engineering control. Engineering controls are devices that physically isolate or remove the hazard.

questions.q: When should sharps containers be replaced?
questions.a: When they are three-quarters (3/4) full — never overfill a sharps container.

questions.q: What should you do if blood splashes into your eyes?
questions.a: Immediately flush the eyes with clean water or saline for at least 15 minutes, then report the incident and seek medical evaluation.

questions.q: What is the best type of glove to use if you are allergic to latex?
questions.a: Nitrile gloves.

questions.q: What must healthcare providers do immediately after removing and discarding used PPE?
questions.a: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water.

questions.q: What are personal eyeglasses considered as in the context of BBP protection?
questions.a: Personal eyeglasses are NOT acceptable as personal protective equipment for bloodborne pathogen protection. Dedicated safety goggles or face shields must be used.

questions.q: What is the first step if you are accidentally exposed to bloodborne pathogens through a cut?
questions.a: Immediately wash the affected area with soap and water, report the incident to your supervisor, and seek medical evaluation.
